input_path: data\papers\Zech et al. - Model-driven Digital Twins for AECO.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The system under study is a **Building Information Model (BIM)-based Digital Twin (DT) for the Architecture, Engineering, Construction, and Operations (AECO) domain**, specifically targeting **building infrastructures** (e.g., academic buildings, commercial facilities, or Living Labs). The DT represents **cyber-physical assets** (e.g., buildings, lighting systems, HVAC, or other Building Control Systems (BCS)) by integrating **static BIM models** (geometric, spatial, and functional representations) with **runtime operational data** (sensor inputs, control logic, and real-time feedback). The DT is designed to enable **life-cycle encompassing optimization**, including phases from **design, construction, operation, refurbishment, and adaptive management** of physical assets. The focus is on **real-time synchronization** between the physical twin (PT) and its virtual counterpart, leveraging **bidirectional communication** for predictive analytics, energy optimization, and user-centered control strategies (e.g., adaptive lighting via reinforcement learning in the BOREALIS project).
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: The DT constellation includes **physical acting components** such as **Building Control Systems (BCS) actuators** (e.g., lighting dimmers, HVAC controllers, motorized shades) and **automated devices** (e.g., IoT-enabled sensors, smart meters, or programmable logic controllers (PLCs)). These components are **modeled within the BIM environment** as part of the **enriched BIM model**, where their **semantic metadata** (e.g., device IDs, endpoints, control logic) is embedded to enable **bidirectional communication** with the DT. The **TwinLight project** specifically addresses **lighting control systems**, integrating **user-defined control strategies** (e.g., occupancy-based dimming, circadian lighting) into the BIM model for automated deployment. The DT can **act on the PT** by sending control signals (e.g., via **Eclipse Hono/Ditto middleware**) to adjust physical systems in real-time, such as modifying lighting levels or HVAC setpoints based on **optimization algorithms** or **reinforcement learning policies** (as demonstrated in BOREALIS).
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: The DT leverages **physical sensing components** including **IoT sensors** (e.g., occupancy sensors, temperature/humidity sensors, luminosity sensors, CO₂ monitors) and **building automation system (BAS) sensors** deployed in the physical asset. These sensors are **modeled in the BIM environment** with **metadata** (e.g., sensor IDs, calibration parameters, data formats) to enable **real-time data acquisition**. The **BOREALIS project** emphasizes **post-occupancy sensor data** (e.g., user presence, environmental conditions) to **iteratively refine control policies** via reinforcement learning. The **Eclipse Hono** middleware acts as a **protocol adapter** for **bidirectional data exchange**, while **Ditto** provides a **digital twin registry** to manage sensor telemetry and device states. Sensor data is **normalized and integrated** into the DT for **real-time monitoring, predictive analytics, and adaptive control** (e.g., energy-efficient lighting adjustments based on occupancy patterns).
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction:
The **physical-to-virtual interaction** is facilitated through **real-time data streams** from the physical asset (PT) to the DT, including:
  1. **Sensor Telemetry**: Raw or processed data from **IoT/BAS sensors** (e.g., temperature, occupancy, luminosity) transmitted via **Eclipse Hono** (using protocols like **MQTT, AMQP, or HTTP**) to the **Ditto digital twin registry**.
  2. **Event-Based Triggers**: Physical events (e.g., door openings, equipment failures) are **mapped to BIM elements** and forwarded to the DT for **real-time processing**.
  3. **Middleware Abstraction**: **Eclipse Hono** acts as a **protocol bridge**, normalizing heterogeneous sensor data into a **unified format** compatible with the DT’s runtime environment. **Ditto** ensures **semantic consistency** by linking sensor data to **BIM entities** (e.g., a temperature sensor tied to a specific room in the BIM model).
  4. **Automated Data Ingestion**: The **model-driven deployment pipeline** extracts **device metadata** (e.g., tenant IDs, endpoint addresses) from the **enriched BIM model** to configure **Hono/Ditto connectors**, enabling **automated data routing** without manual intervention.
  The interaction is **bidirectional** but **asynchronous**, with data processed in near-real-time (latency depends on sensor update rates and network conditions).
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction:
The **virtual-to-physical interaction** is enabled through **control signals** generated by the DT and transmitted back to the **Building Control Systems (BCS)** or **IoT actuators** in the physical asset. This includes:
  1. **Actuator Commands**: The DT generates **control commands** (e.g., lighting dimming levels, HVAC setpoints) based on **optimization algorithms** (e.g., reinforcement learning in BOREALIS) or **user-defined rules** (e.g., occupancy-based scheduling in TwinLight). These commands are **serialized and routed** via **Eclipse Hono/Ditto** to the target actuators.
  2. **Bidirectional Communication**: The **Ditto digital twin registry** maintains **state synchronization** between the virtual and physical components, ensuring that **control actions** are reflected in both the DT and PT.
  3. **Middleware Orchestration**: **Eclipse Hono** handles **protocol translation** (e.g., converting DT commands to **Modbus, BACnet, or OPC UA** for legacy BCS integration), while **Ditto** manages **device lifecycles** (e.g., firmware updates, fault detection).
  4. **Real-Time Feedback Loop**: The DT continuously **monitors the impact** of virtual commands on the PT (e.g., via sensor feedback) to **adjust control policies dynamically**, closing the **cyber-physical loop**.
  The interaction is **event-driven** and **deterministic**, with **low-latency execution** critical for **safety-critical systems** (e.g., emergency lighting).
extracted_characteristics.dt_services:
The DT provides the following **services** to users and the physical system:
  1. **Real-Time Monitoring**: **Interactive 3D visualization** (via **Unreal Engine 5**) of building operations, including **sensor telemetry overlays** (e.g., temperature maps, occupancy heatmaps) and **alerts** (e.g., equipment failures).
  2. **Predictive Analytics**: **Data-driven optimization** of building performance (e.g., energy consumption, thermal comfort) using **machine learning** (e.g., reinforcement learning in BOREALIS) or **rule-based engines** (e.g., TwinLight’s lighting control policies).
  3. **Adaptive Control**: **Dynamic adjustment** of BCS parameters (e.g., lighting levels, HVAC schedules) based on **real-time sensor data** and **occupancy patterns**, with **user-defined constraints** (e.g., energy savings targets).
  4. **Simulation and What-If Analysis**: **Digital twin entanglement (ODTE) metric**-based evaluation of **scenario testing** (e.g., 'What if occupancy increases by 20%?') to optimize **design and operational decisions**.
  5. **Service Extensibility**: **Modular architecture** with **standardized extension points** (e.g., plugins for custom analytics, third-party integrations) to support **scalable DT evolution** (e.g., adding **fire safety simulations** or **acoustic modeling**).
  6. **Facility Management Tools**: **Role-based access** for **Building Control Engineers** (device configuration), **Facility Managers** (operational oversight), and **DT Engineers** (service development), with **audit logs** for compliance.
  Services are **orchestrated** via the **DT Framework**, which acts as a **middleware layer** between the **BIM model**, **runtime data**, and **user interfaces** (e.g., Unreal Engine dashboards).
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale:
The **twinning time scale** spans **real-time to long-term adaptive cycles**, with the following characteristics:
  1. **Real-Time Synchronization**: Data exchange between PT and DT occurs at **sub-second to millisecond intervals** for **critical control loops** (e.g., lighting adjustments, HVAC responses), enabled by **Eclipse Hono/Ditto’s low-latency messaging**.
  2. **Operational Time Scales**: **Hourly/daily updates** for **energy optimization** (e.g., BOREALIS’s reinforcement learning policies) and **occupancy-based scheduling**.
  3. **Long-Term Evolution**: **Iterative refinement** of DT models and control strategies over **weeks to years**, leveraging **post-occupancy data** (e.g., sensor logs) to **adapt to changing usage patterns** (e.g., seasonal variations, renovations).
  4. **Event-Driven Triggers**: **Asynchronous updates** for **exceptional conditions** (e.g., sensor failures, emergency shutdowns), with **automated recovery mechanisms** (e.g., fallback control modes).
  The **Overall Digital Twin Entanglement (ODTE) metric** is used to **quantify synchronization fidelity** over time, ensuring the DT remains **operationally relevant** across all scales.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities:
The DT system supports **multiplicities** through a **modular, service-oriented architecture** with two primary deployment patterns:
  1. **Centralized DT**: A **single, unified digital twin** representing the entire building infrastructure, with **shared runtime artifacts** (e.g., Ditto registry, Hono middleware) and **global state management**. This approach is **scalable for small-to-medium buildings** and ensures **consistent data integrity** via **BIM’s semantic backbone**.
  2. **Decentralized/Composite DTs**: **Sub-twins** for **specific building zones or systems** (e.g., a DT for lighting, another for HVAC), each with **localized control logic** but **interoperable via Ditto’s digital twin registry**. This enables **fine-grained optimization** (e.g., BOREALIS’s zone-specific lighting policies) while maintaining **global coherence**.
  The framework supports **hybrid multiplicities**, where **core services** (e.g., real-time monitoring) are centralized, while **specialized services** (e.g., adaptive control) are **decentralized** for **performance and autonomy**. **Model-to-model transformations** automate the **composition of sub-twins** from the **enriched BIM model**, ensuring **semantic consistency** across multiplicities.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages:
The DT operates across **five key life-cycle stages**, aligned with the **AECO domain**:
  1. **Design Phase**: The DT is **initialized from the BIM model**, incorporating **static geometric and functional data** (e.g., building layout, MEP systems). **Device modeling** (e.g., sensors, actuators) is performed by **Building Control Engineers** to define **runtime capabilities**.
  2. **Construction Phase**: The DT **evolves with as-built data**, integrating **real-world deviations** (e.g., material substitutions, installation errors) via **BIM coordination tools**. **Commissioning** is automated using **Hono/Ditto connectors** to link **physical devices** to their **virtual counterparts**.
  3. **Operation Phase**: The DT enables **real-time monitoring and control**, with **facility managers** using **Unreal Engine dashboards** for **interactive oversight**. **Adaptive services** (e.g., BOREALIS’s reinforcement learning) **optimize performance** based on **occupancy and environmental data**.
  4. **Refurbishment Phase**: The DT **supports retrofitting** by **updating BIM models** with **renovation plans** and **simulating impact** (e.g., energy savings from new HVAC systems) before physical implementation.
  5. **Decommissioning/End-of-Life**: The DT **archives operational data** for **post-occupancy evaluation** (e.g., lifecycle cost analysis) and **facilitates asset repurposing** (e.g., adaptive reuse simulations).
  The DT **covers representations** of the PT as:
  - **As-designed (ideal)**: Initial BIM model with **design intent**.
  - **As-manufactured**: **As-built BIM** with **construction deviations**.
  - **As-operated**: **Runtime data-enriched BIM** reflecting **real-world usage patterns**.
  The **Overall Digital Twin Entanglement (ODTE) metric** is used to **validate fidelity** across all stages.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data:
The DT comprises the following **models and data components**:
  1. **BIM Core Model**: A **semantically enriched IFC (Industry Foundation Classes) model** containing **geometric, spatial, and functional data** (e.g., building elements, MEP systems, zoning).
  2. **Runtime Device Models**: **Metadata-extended BIM entities** representing **sensors, actuators, and controllers**, including:
     - **Device IDs** (e.g., Ditto’s `thingId`).
     - **Endpoint addresses** (e.g., MQTT topics, HTTP endpoints).
     - **Control logic** (e.g., TwinLight’s lighting policies, BOREALIS’s RL policies).
  3. **Digital Twin Registry (Ditto)**: A **centralized repository** for **digital twin instances**, storing:
     - **Live telemetry** (e.g., sensor readings, actuator states).
     - **Historical data** (e.g., time-series logs for analytics).
     - **Policy configurations** (e.g., energy-saving thresholds).
  4. **Visualization Models**: **Unreal Engine 5 assets** (e.g., 3D meshes, shaders) for **interactive rendering** of building operations, with **real-time data overlays**.
  5. **Optimization Models**: **Machine learning models** (e.g., BOREALIS’s reinforcement learning agents) or **rule-based engines** (e.g., TwinLight’s control strategies) embedded in the DT for **autonomous decision-making**.
  6. **Middleware Artifacts**: **Eclipse Hono configurations** for **protocol adaptation** (e.g., MQTT, AMQP) and **Ditto policies** for **access control** and **data validation**.
  Data is **structured** using **BIM standards (IFC, COBie)** and **IoT interoperability frameworks (OPC UA, OneM2M)**, ensuring **semantic consistency** across the DT constellation.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers:
The DT leverages the following **tooling and enablers** to achieve its goals:
  1. **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) Tools**:
     - **BIM Authoring Tools** (e.g., Autodesk Revit, Graphisoft ArchiCAD) for **enriched BIM model creation**.
     - **Model Transformation Engines** (e.g., **Eclipse Modeling Framework (EMF)**, **Acceleo**) to **generate runtime artifacts** (e.g., Hono/Ditto configurations) from BIM.
  2. **Middleware Platforms**:
     - **Eclipse Hono**: Provides **protocol adaptation** and **device connectivity** for **heterogeneous IoT/BAS systems**, supporting **MQTT, AMQP, and HTTP**.
     - **Eclipse Ditto**: Acts as a **digital twin registry** with **CRUD operations** for twin instances, **event sourcing**, and **state management**.
  3. **Visualization Engine**:
     - **Unreal Engine 5**: Enables **real-time 3D visualization** with **data-driven animations** (e.g., sensor heatmaps, control feedback).
  4. **Automation Frameworks**:
     - **Model-to-Model (M2M) and Model-to-Text (M2T) transformations** to **automate DT deployment** from BIM, reducing manual effort.
     - **CI/CD Pipelines** for **continuous integration** of **custom services** (e.g., new analytics plugins).
  5. **Evaluation Metrics**:
     - **Overall Digital Twin Entanglement (ODTE) metric** to **quantify synchronization fidelity** between PT and DT.
  6. **Domain-Specific Languages (DSLs)**:
     - **Custom metamodels** for **BCS runtime aspects** (e.g., control logic, sensor configurations) embedded in BIM.
  The toolchain is **open-source** (where applicable) and **interoperable**, with **standardized extension points** for **third-party integrations** (e.g., **BACnet, OPC UA**).
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation:
The **DT constellation** is a **service-oriented architecture** orchestrating the following components:
  1. **BIM Backbone**: The **central semantic model** providing **geometric, spatial, and functional context** for all DT services.
  2. **Runtime Middleware Layer**:
     - **Eclipse Hono**: Handles **device connectivity** and **protocol translation**.
     - **Eclipse Ditto**: Manages **digital twin instances**, **state synchronization**, and **event routing**.
  3. **Control Plane**:
     - **Building Control Engineers** define **device configurations** and **control logic** within the BIM model.
     - **DT Engineers** develop **custom services** (e.g., analytics, simulations) and integrate them via **standardized extension points**.
  4. **User Interaction Layer**:
     - **Facility Managers** interact with the DT via **Unreal Engine dashboards** for **real-time monitoring and control**.
     - **Role-based access control** ensures **secure, granular permissions**.
  5. **Data Plane**:
     - **Sensor telemetry** flows from PT to DT via **Hono/Ditto**.
     - **Control commands** flow from DT to PT via **middleware-adapted protocols**.
  6. **Extensibility Layer**:
     - **Plugins and microservices** (e.g., **energy optimization**, **predictive maintenance**) can be **dynamically added** without redeploying the core DT.
  The constellation is **modular and scalable**, with **loose coupling** between components to enable **independent evolution**. **Model-driven deployment** ensures **consistent configuration** across the constellation.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution:
The **twinning process** follows a **model-driven, iterative lifecycle** with the following phases:
  1. **Requirements Elicitation**:
     - **Stakeholder roles** (Building Control Engineers, Facility Managers, DT Engineers) define **functional and non-functional requirements** (e.g., real-time latency, energy savings targets).
     - **BIM model enrichment** with **device metadata** (e.g., sensor/actuator specifications, control policies).
  2. **Model Transformation**:
     - **Automated extraction** of **runtime artifacts** (e.g., Hono/Ditto configurations) from the **enriched BIM model** using **MDE tools**.
     - **Validation** of the BIM model against **semantic constraints** (e.g., device connectivity, control logic consistency).
  3. **Deployment Automation**:
     - **Hono/Ditto Configurator** scans the BIM model for **‘twinnable’ devices** and **generates middleware bindings**.
     - **Unreal Engine assets** are **auto-generated** for visualization.
  4. **Runtime Initialization**:
     - **Bidirectional communication** is established between PT and DT via **Hono/Ditto**.
     - **Initial synchronization** of **BIM data** and **sensor telemetry**.
  5. **Operation and Evolution**:
     - **Facility Managers** monitor and **adjust control policies** via the DT.
     - **DT Engineers** **extend functionality** through **custom services** (e.g., new analytics modules).
     - **Post-occupancy data** (e.g., sensor logs) is used to **refine control strategies** (e.g., BOREALIS’s RL policies).
  6. **Quality Assurance**:
     - **Overall Digital Twin Entanglement (ODTE) metric** is used to **validate synchronization fidelity**.
     - **Automated testing** ensures **interoperability** and **fault tolerance**.
  **Milestones** include:
  - **Initial Deployment**: DT is **operationally ready** within **weeks** of BIM model preparation.
  - **Continuous Updates**: **Incremental upgrades** (e.g., new sensors, control policies) via **runtime configuration management**.
  - **Major Revisions**: **Full redeployment** triggered by **architectural changes** (e.g., building renovations).
  The process is **documented** using **model-driven artifacts** (e.g., metamodels, transformation scripts) to ensure **reproducibility** and **maintainability**.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations:
The **fidelity and validity** of the DT are ensured through:
  1. **Semantic Consistency**:
     - **BIM’s IFC schema** provides a **standardized foundation** for **geometric and functional data**.
     - **Model transformations** enforce **mapping rules** between BIM entities and **runtime artifacts** (e.g., Ditto twins).
  2. **Data Validation**:
     - **Middleware (Hono/Ditto)** includes **data validation policies** (e.g., range checks for sensor readings, format validation for control commands).
     - **Automated reconciliation** between **BIM data** and **sensor telemetry** to detect **discrepancies** (e.g., misplaced sensors).
  3. **Synchronization Metrics**:
     - **Overall Digital Twin Entanglement (ODTE) metric** quantifies **alignment** between PT and DT, with **thresholds** for **acceptable drift**.
     - **Event-based synchronization** ensures **low-latency updates** for **critical systems** (e.g., emergency lighting).
  4. **Uncertainty Handling**:
     - **Probabilistic models** (e.g., Bayesian inference) are used to **estimate sensor errors** or **model inaccuracies**.
     - **Fallback mechanisms** (e.g., manual overrides) are provided for **high-risk scenarios**.
  5. **Verification and Validation (V&V)**:
     - **Unit testing** of **individual services** (e.g., control logic, visualization).
     - **Integration testing** of **Hono/Ditto middleware** with **BIM and physical devices**.
     - **User acceptance testing (UAT)** with **Facility Managers** to validate **usability and operational relevance**.
  6. **Error Recovery**:
     - **Automated alerts** for **data anomalies** (e.g., sensor failures).
     - **Graceful degradation** (e.g., switching to **predefined control modes**) during **network outages**.
  The DT’s **validity** is **continuously monitored** via **ODTE metrics** and **stakeholder feedback**, with **corrective actions** triggered for **deviations beyond thresholds**.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection:
The **technical connection** between the **Physical Twin (PT)** and **Digital Twin (DT)** is established via:
  1. **Network Protocols**:
     - **Eclipse Hono** supports **MQTT (v3.1.1/v5.0)**, **AMQP 1.0**, and **HTTP/REST** for **device connectivity**.
     - **OPC UA** and **BACnet** are used for **legacy BCS integration**.
  2. **Architecture**:
     - **Hybrid cloud-edge deployment**: **Hono/Ditto** can run on **edge devices** (e.g., Raspberry Pi) for **low-latency control** or in the **cloud** for **scalability**.
     - **Service mesh** (e.g., **Istio, Linkerd**) for **secure inter-service communication** within the DT constellation.
  3. **Data Flow**:
     - **Sensor → Hono → Ditto → DT Services**: Raw data is **normalized** by Hono and **stored/processed** by Ditto before being **consumed by analytics or visualization**.
     - **DT Services → Hono → Actuators**: Control commands are **serialized** by Ditto and **routed** via Hono to **physical devices**.
  4. **Latency Requirements**:
     - **Critical control loops** (e.g., lighting, HVAC) require **<100ms latency**.
     - **Non-critical updates** (e.g., energy reports) tolerate **higher latency** (e.g., **minutes to hours**).
  5. **Security**:
     - **TLS 1.3** for **encrypted communication**.
     - **Role-based access control (RBAC)** in Ditto for **data isolation**.
  The connection is **resilient** to **network partitions** via **eventual consistency** models in Ditto and **retry mechanisms** in Hono.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment:
The **DT hosting and deployment** follows a **modular, scalable approach**:
  1. **Deployment Models**:
     - **On-Premises**: DT runs on **local servers** for **high-security environments** (e.g., government buildings).
     - **Cloud**: **Containerized deployment** (e.g., **Docker, Kubernetes**) on **public/private clouds** (e.g., AWS, Azure) for **scalability**.
     - **Edge Computing**: **Lightweight DT instances** deployed on **IoT gateways** (e.g., **Raspberry Pi, NVIDIA Jetson**) for **low-latency control**.
  2. **Infrastructure**:
     - **Kubernetes clusters** for **orchestrating microservices** (e.g., Ditto, Hono, custom plugins).
     - **Message brokers** (e.g., **Apache Kafka, RabbitMQ**) for **high-throughput data streaming**.
  3. **CI/CD Pipeline**:
     - **GitOps** workflows for **automated deployments** from **BIM model changes**.
     - **Blue-green deployments** to **minimize downtime** during updates.
  4. **Scalability**:
     - **Horizontal scaling** of **Ditto instances** for **large buildings** with **thousands of sensors**.
     - **Auto-scaling** of **Unreal Engine render nodes** for **high-resolution visualization**.
  5. **Cost Optimization**:
     - **Serverless functions** (e.g., AWS Lambda) for **event-driven services** (e.g., alert processing).
     - **Spot instances** for **non-critical analytics**.
  The deployment is **automated** via **model-driven scripts**, reducing **manual effort** to **<10% of total deployment time** (as demonstrated in the Living Lab case study).
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making:
The DT generates **actionable insights** and supports **decision-making** through:
  1. **Real-Time Alerts**:
     - **Anomaly detection** (e.g., **unexpected energy spikes**, **equipment failures**) via **statistical thresholds** or **machine learning**.
     - **Visual cues** in **Unreal Engine dashboards** (e.g., **red-highlighted zones** for temperature violations).
  2. **Predictive Analytics**:
     - **Energy consumption forecasts** based on **historical sensor data** and **occupancy patterns**.
     - **Failure prediction** for **HVAC or lighting systems** using **time-series analysis**.
  3. **Optimization Recommendations**:
     - **Automated suggestions** for **control policy adjustments** (e.g., 'Reduce lighting in Zone A by 15% to save 20% energy').
     - **Cost-benefit analysis** for **retrofitting decisions** (e.g., 'Upgrading to LED lighting in Zone B will reduce costs by $X/year').
  4. **Scenario Testing**:
     - **What-if simulations** (e.g., 'How will occupancy changes affect energy use?') using **ODTE-validated models**.
     - **A/B testing** of **control strategies** (e.g., comparing **occupancy-based vs. time-based lighting**).
  5. **Compliance Reporting**:
     - **Automated generation** of **energy certification reports** (e.g., LEED, BREEAM).
     - **Audit trails** for **regulatory compliance** (e.g., **GDPR, ISO 55000**).
  Insights are **delivered** via:
  - **Unreal Engine dashboards** for **visual exploration**.
  - **APIs** for **third-party integrations** (e.g., **ERP systems, CMMS**).
  - **Email/SMS alerts** for **critical decisions** (e.g., **maintenance scheduling**).
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration:
The DT supports **horizontal integration** with **external information systems** through:
  1. **Standardized Data Exchange**:
     - **IFC (Industry Foundation Classes)** for **BIM interoperability** with **design tools** (e.g., Revit, ArchiCAD).
     - **COBie (Construction Operations Building Information Exchange)** for **facility management data**.
     - **OPC UA** for **integration with industrial automation systems**.
  2. **API Gateways**:
     - **RESTful APIs** for **third-party applications** (e.g., **CMMS, ERP, energy management systems**).
     - **GraphQL** for **flexible data querying** (e.g., 'Fetch all sensors in Zone X with temperature > 25°C').
  3. **Event-Driven Architectures**:
     - **Webhooks** for **real-time notifications** to **external systems** (e.g., **alerting platforms like PagerDuty**).
     - **Kafka topics** for **high-throughput data streaming** to **analytics engines** (e.g., **Spark, Flink**).
  4. **Legacy System Integration**:
     - **ETL pipelines** to **ingest data** from **proprietary BCS** (e.g., **Johnson Controls, Siemens Desigo**).
     - **Middleware adapters** (e.g., **Hono plugins**) for **custom protocols**.
  5. **Cross-DT Collaboration**:
     - **Federated DTs** for **multi-building campuses** (e.g., **university complexes**) using **Ditto’s multi-tenancy**.
     - **Shared data lakes** (e.g., **Delta Lake, Apache Iceberg**) for **centralized analytics** across DTs.
  Integration is **governed** by **semantic web standards** (e.g., **RDF, OWL**) to ensure **interoperability** with **external ontologies** (e.g., **BIM-related standards like IfcOWL**).
extracted_characteristics.data_ownership_and_privacy:
The DT addresses **data ownership and privacy** through:
  1. **Ownership Model**:
     - **Physical Twin (PT) Owner**: Retains **primary ownership** of **operational data** (e.g., sensor telemetry, control logs).
     - **DT Service Provider**: Manages **runtime infrastructure** (e.g., Hono/Ditto middleware) but **does not own** raw data.
     - **Data Sharing Agreements**: **Role-based access** is enforced via **Ditto’s RBAC**, with **audit logs** for **compliance tracking**.
  2. **Privacy Measures**:
     - **Data Minimization**: Only **necessary sensor data** is collected (e.g., **occupancy counts** instead of **individual identities**).
     - **Anonymization**: **Pseudonymization** of **user-related data** (e.g., **occupancy patterns** without personal IDs).
     - **GDPR Compliance**: **Right to erasure** implemented via **Ditto’s data deletion APIs**.
  3. **Technical Safeguards**:
     - **Encryption at Rest**: **AES-256** for **stored data** (e.g., sensor logs in Ditto).
     - **Encryption in Transit**: **TLS 1.3** for **all network communications**.
     - **Token-Based Authentication**: **OAuth 2.0/OIDC** for **user access control**.
  4. **Ethical Considerations**:
     - **Bias Mitigation**: **Algorithmic fairness** checks for **control policies** (e.g., ensuring **reinforcement learning models** do not discriminate based on **occupancy patterns**).
     - **Transparency**: **Explainable AI** techniques for **decision-making** (e.g., **SHAP values** for RL policy explanations).
  Data governance is **documented** in **model-driven artifacts** (e.g., **metamodels for access policies**) and **audited** via **automated compliance checks** (e.g., **GDPR, ISO 27001**).
extracted_characteristics.standardization:
The DT adheres to the following **standards** for **engineering and interoperability**:
  1. **BIM Standards**:
     - **Industry Foundation Classes (IFC)**: For **geometric and functional data exchange**.
     - **COBie**: For **facility management data**.
     - **IfcOWL**: **Ontological representation** of BIM data for **semantic reasoning**.
  2. **IoT and Automation Standards**:
     - **OPC UA**: For **machine-to-machine communication** with **industrial systems**.
     - **MQTT (OASIS)**: For **lightweight IoT messaging**.
     - **OneM2M**: For **cross-domain IoT interoperability**.
  3. **Digital Twin Standards**:
     - **DMTF’s Digital Twin Standards**: For **twin lifecycle management**.
     - **ISO 23227**: For **digital twin interoperability**.
  4. **Middleware Standards**:
     - **Eclipse Hono’s IoT Protocol Adapters**: For **standardized device connectivity**.
     - **Eclipse Ditto’s Digital Twin Registry**: Aligns with **W3C’s Web of Things (WoT)** standards.
  5. **Software Engineering Standards**:
     - **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE)**: Uses **EMF, Acceleo** for **model transformations**.
     - **Microservices Architecture**: Follows **CNCF best practices** for **containerized deployments**.
  6. **Security Standards**:
     - **NIST SP 800-53**: For **cybersecurity controls**.
     - **ISO 27001**: For **information security management**.
  The framework **extends existing standards** where necessary (e.g., **custom metamodels for BCS runtime aspects**) while **ensuring backward compatibility** with **legacy systems** (e.g., **BACnet, KNX**).
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations:
The DT incorporates **security and safety considerations** through:
  1. **Cybersecurity Measures**:
     - **Network Segmentation**: **Zero-trust architecture** with **micro-segmentation** (e.g., **Calico, Cilium**) to **isolate DT components**.
     - **Intrusion Detection**: **Anomaly monitoring** (e.g., **Zeek, Suricata**) for **unauthorized access attempts**.
     - **Secure Boot**: **Immutable firmware** for **edge devices** (e.g., **IoT gateways**) to prevent **tampering**.
  2. **Safety Mechanisms**:
     - **Fail-Safe Controls**: **Hardware watchdogs** on **edge devices** to **reset faulty actuators**.
     - **Redundancy**: **Hot standby** for **critical services** (e.g., **emergency lighting control**).
     - **Safety Instrumented Systems (SIS)**: **Independent safety loops** for **high-risk operations** (e.g., **HVAC shutdowns**).
  3. **Operational Safety**:
     - **Remote Operation Limits**: **Geofencing** to **prevent unauthorized remote control** (e.g., **only allow adjustments from within the building’s network**).
     - **Human-in-the-Loop**: **Manual override** for **safety-critical decisions** (e.g., **fire alarm triggers**).
     - **Safety Audits**: **Automated checks** for **control logic flaws** (e.g., **deadlocks in reinforcement learning policies**).
  4. **Compliance**:
     - **ISO 13849**: For **safety-related control systems**.
     - **IEC 61508**: For **functional safety** in **industrial automation**.
     - **NIST IR 8259**: For **supply chain risk management** in **IoT deployments**.
  5. **Incident Response**:
     - **Automated Rollback**: **Versioned control policies** to **revert to safe states** after failures.
     - **Forensic Logging**: **Immutable logs** for **post-incident analysis** (e.g., **blockchain-backed audit trails**).
  Safety is **validated** via **formal methods** (e.g., **model checking**) for **control logic** and **penetration testing** for **cybersecurity**. The DT **avoids remote execution** of **safety-critical operations** unless **explicitly authorized** by **on-site personnel**.

# Model-driven Digital Twins for AECO

Philipp Zech _[∗]_, Simon Senoner _[∗]_, Emanuele Goldin _[∗]_, Christoph Zallinger _[∗]_, Sascha Hammes _[†]_, Judith Michael _[‡]_

_∗_ Department of Computer Science, University of Innsbruck, Austria

_†_ Unit of Energy Efficient Buildings, University of Innsbruck, Austria

_‡_ University of Regensburg, Programming and Software Engineering, Germany
Emails: _{_ firstname.lastname _}_ @uibk.ac.at, judith.michael@ur.de



_**Abstract**_ **—Digital Twins (DTs) are becoming central to the**
**architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO)**
**domain, enabling real-time monitoring, simulation, and life cycle-**
**encompassing optimization of building operations through unified**
**virtual representations of physical assets. However, practical**
**deployment remains challenging due to fragmented data sources,**
**domain-specific engineering workflows, and the absence of stan-**
**dardized integration frameworks. To address these issues, we**
**present a model-driven framework for scalable DT deploy-**
**ment, grounded in Building Information Modeling (BIM). Our**
**approach automates the extraction of domain semantics and**
**the generation of runtime interfaces, thereby reducing manual**
**effort and enhancing maintainability. The framework integrates**
**Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange and leverages**
**Unreal Engine for rich, interactive visualization. It also features**
**standardized extension points to enable modular, service-oriented**
**DT platforms. We demonstrate the practical application of**
**our approach in a Living Lab case study and evaluate the**
**entanglement of the resulting DT using the Overall Digital Twin**
**Entanglement (ODTE) metric, underscoring its interoperability**
**and architectural soundness.**
_**Index Terms**_ **—Model-driven Engineering, Model-driven De-**
**ployment, Building Information Modeling, Digital Twins, Unreal**
**Engine**


I. I NTRODUCTION


Digital Twins (DT) are cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static
models with runtime data creates a virtual representation
of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization [1], [2]. Crucially, in the
architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO)
domain, DTs enable data-driven infrastructure management
and optimization by providing real-time insights, predictive
analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities [3]–[7].
This transformation promises tangible benefits such as reduced
operational costs, enhanced decision-making, and improved
stakeholder collaboration along the complete building life
cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations
during operation [2], [8]–[12]. However, the realization of this
potential substantially hinges on the ability to systematically
and efficiently deploy DTs in complex and heterogeneous
infrastructure environments [11]–[13].
Despite their promise, the deployment of infrastructure DTs
remains elusive in practice [2]. This is primarily due to
the fragmented nature of models and data, domain-specific


This research has received funding from the Austrian Research Promotion
Agency (FFG) under Grant Agreement No.: 898708, TwinLight, and Grant
Agreement No.: 910225, BOREALIS.



engineering workflows, and a lack of standardized integration mechanisms [7], [14]. These challenges lead to highly
customized and siloed solutions that are difficult to scale
or replicate across projects. Without a cohesive deployment
workflow, the AECO domain faces a bottleneck where the
cost and complexity of DT implementations outweigh the
perceived benefits, thus stifling broader adoption and DTdriven innovation [2], [7].
This raises a pressing question: _how can we deploy main-_
_tainable and interoperable DTs in AECO at scale, despite_
_complications from heterogeneous models, fragmented data_
_landscapes, and entrenched engineering practices?_ As the
construction industry stands at the intersection of digital innovation and practical implementation, answering this question
is critical for unlocking the full potential of DTs and driving
meaningful, domain-wide transformation and innovation.
In this paper, we propose a model-driven deployment framework that leverages Building Information Modeling (BIM) as a
semantic backbone for automating the creation, configuration,
and deployment of DTs in AECO. Our approach systematically extracts relevant asset data from a BIM model and
generates runtime interfaces for bidirectional communication.
This enables end-to-end automation of the DT pipeline —
apart from initial building modeling and building-site device
configuration — thus significantly reducing manual effort. By
integrating with Eclipse Hono [15] and Ditto [16], our proposal supports real-time data exchange, while Unreal Engine
5 [17] provides a rich visualization environment for interactive
monitoring and simulation. Crucially, our platform features
standardized extension points for modular service integration,
supporting scalable deployment and future extensibility. We
demonstrate the practical viability of our proposal through a
DT of a Living Lab, and by measuring how well the resulting
DT reflects its physical counterpart using the Overall Digital
Twin Entanglement (ODTE) metric [18].
**Organization.** Section II outlines the research context of
our work. Section III defines BIM’s role and introduces requirements for model-driven deployment framework for DTs.
Section IV details our solution. Section V presents the case
study and evaluation, and discusses results and limitations.
Section VI concludes our paper.


II. R ESEARCH C ONTEXT AND P ROBLEM


The work presented in this paper is rooted in two ongoing
research projects, viz. TwinLight [19] and BOREALIS [20],
which together establish the research context for model-driven


DT deployment in the built environment. Both projects explore
how runtime semantics, such as sensor configurations, control
logic, and live operational data can be systematically embedded into BIM models, transforming them from static planning
artifacts into dynamic, runtime-capable digital representations,
cf. DTs.

TwinLight addresses the fragmentation and opacity of current building automation workflows by introducing a BIMcentric control configuration framework. This framework aims
to improve system transparency and interoperability across
construction phases and stakeholders by embedding domainspecific control semantics for lighting systems directly into
BIM models. To this end, TwinLight proposes a layered system
architecture that couples BIM with a middleware for automated commissioning, bidirectional communication between
virtual and physical assets, and seamless integration of nontrivial, user-centered control strategies [9], [21].
BOREALIS, in contrast, focuses on adaptive lighting systems using reinforcement learning to balance and optimize
energy consumption, visual comfort, and non-visual health
effects. Its core innovation lies in using post-occupancy sensor
data to iteratively improve control policies over time, thus
closing performance gaps between design and operation. Here,
BIM serves not only as the model backbone for spatial and
semantic data integration, but also as a ”digital RL environment” where control models and sensor feedback converge.
BOREALIS exemplifies how runtime aspects can be both
engineered and evolved within a BIM-based DT ecosystem.
Together, these projects provide a practical motivation for
this paper’s proposal: a model-driven deployment framework
that bridges the gap between BIM modeling and the operational realities of infrastructure DTs.


_A. Related Work_


DTs are increasingly recognized in the AECO domain
as to their potential in enabling real-time synchronization
between physical assets and their digital counterparts. Recent
research highlights the integration of DTs with BIM, artificial intelligence, and sensor networks to enhance life cycle
management, cost modeling, and operational efficiency [7],

[14], [22]–[24]. Current literature emphasizes the role of
modeling and workflow automation in achieving consistent
and scalable DT implementations, particularly for modular
construction and infrastructure monitoring [6], [13], [25], [26].
Despite its promise, DT adoption remains limited by fragmented, case-specific deployments that often lack standardized
data schemas, robust integration frameworks, and bidirectional
communication protocols [7], [22], [25], [27]. These technical
challenges are compounded by organizational barriers, including insufficient stakeholder alignment, high implementation
costs, and the absence of unified engineering workflows [23],

[28], [29]. Addressing these limitations will require more
than technological advancement by demanding a rethinking
of system and tool integration, stakeholder collaboration, and
governance across the asset life cycle [13].
As for open source DT frameworks, Gil et al. [30] identify 14 frameworks, most of which target industrial or IoT



applications and offer infrastructure-level components rather
than complete, deployable systems. While frameworks like
Eclipse Ditto [16] and BaSyx [31] provide robust connectivity and standard compliance, they require significant manual
configuration, integration of analytics, and orchestration of
components. Out-of-the-box functionality is generally limited,
and usability for non-developers is low.
In the AECO sector, the situation is more restricted [2],

[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

Recently, Gerber et al. [33] presented an ad-hoc DT for
a Living Lab for a LEED Platinum academic building based
on BIM for monitoring, simulation, and performance analysis.
Their study emphasizes automated data acquisition, normalization, and 3D visualization, while highlighting challenges
in data integration and stakeholder coordination, insights that
directly inform model-driven approaches in DT engineering.
Their results reinforce earlier findings by Opoku et al. [34]
who also demonstrated the effective integration of DTs with
BIM and IoT platforms to optimize indoor environmental conditions in a university library setting. Both studies underscore
the significance of real-time data utilization and visualization
in enhancing facility management and highlight common challenges in data integration and stakeholder engagement in DT
implementations. In synopsis, AECO adoption is dominated by
commercial tools or custom-built solutions [2], [13]. Notably,
MDE is not present across the surveyed works, leaving a
critical gap in abstraction and reuse capabilities essential for
complex domains like AECO.
Model-driven engineering (MDE) offers a streamlined approach to handling the increasing complexity of systems by
elevating models to primary engineering artifacts [35], [36].
It enables abstraction, formalization, and automation across
the development life cycle, reducing cognitive overload and
improving consistency in large-scale systems [37]. MDE supports the definition of domain-specific modeling languages
and metamodels that unify engineering workflows, providing
a structured foundation for communication across disciplines
and life cycle phases [38], [39]. Importantly, MDE also
strengthens governance by encoding organizational policies
and architectural decisions into formal models, promoting
compliance, reuse, and quality assurance [40]. Recent advances continue to refine the integration of MDE into DT
systems [41]–[47]. As reported in Lehner et al.’s recent survey,
research primarily focuses on runtime adaptability, semantic
coherence, and tool interoperability [48]. Abbasi et al. [49]
examined the phenomenon of _semantic drift_, where the alignment between digital models and their physical counterparts
diverges over time. Their study stresses the necessity of
synchronization techniques to ensure the integrity of modelbased DTs. Kamburjan et al. [50] proposed a declarative life
cycle specification language, enabling dynamic evolution and
rule-based management of DT instances. Meanwhile, Oakes et
al. [51] advanced ontological frameworks to support service

oriented DT architectures, improving semantic integration
across platforms. Giulianelli et al. [52] introduced a webbased approach to engineer interoperable ecosystems of DTs,
facilitating seamless integration and communication across
diverse platforms. These contributions underscore the evolving
landscape of DT engineering, where MDE-inspired methodologies are pivotal in managing both growing complexity and
dynamic behaviors in cyber-physical systems [53].
Recently, the application of MDE for engineering DTs of
buildings has received first attention [9], [11], [12], [21],

[54]–[56]. Specifically, the integration of BIM with MDE for
automating the deployment of DTs for buildings has been
proposed [9], [11], [12], [21], [56], [57]. These approaches
show promising results by directly leveraging BIM models for
model-to-model and model-to-text based transformations [58]
for generating runtime artifacts for the (semi-)automatic establishment of DTs. The current work aims at streamlining
these results into a BIM-based, model-driven framework for
the automated deployment of DTs of building infrastructures.


_B. Challenges and Contributions_


Despite increasing interest in, and ongoing research efforts
on DTs in the AECO domain, several systemic challenges
( **C** _X_ ) hinder their widespread deployment as elaborated so far:


_•_ **C1:** BIM data is fragmented and semantically heterogeneous, hindering unified interpretation across disciplines.

_•_ **C2:** DT deployment remains largely manual, resulting in
inconsistent setups and high engineering overhead.

_•_ **C3:** Existing DT frameworks lack seamless, real-time
interaction between virtual models and physical systems.

_•_ **C4:** Current DT implementations offer limited extensibility, making it difficult to integrate or evolve services.

In response ( **R** _X_ ) to these challenges, this work introduces a
model-driven DT deployment framework grounded in BIM,
which advances the state-of-the-art through the following
contributions:


_•_ **R1:** A modeling methodology to standardize BIM semantics for a model-driven transformation pipeline to
generate consistent runtime artifacts ( **C1** ).

_•_ **R2:** An automated deployment process that reduces manual intervention and enables reproducible DT setups ( **C2** ).

_•_ **R3:** Real-time data integration using Eclipse Hono and
Ditto, enabling live synchronization between physical
assets and digital twins ( **C3** ).

_•_ **R4:** A modular architecture with defined extension points
to support scalable, service-oriented DT development in
AECO ( **C4** ).

Together, these contributions aim to operationalize scalable
and interoperable DTs in AECO contexts. By closing the gap
between design-time models and runtime operations, we offer
a sustainable path toward domain-wide DT adoption.
Our work is organized using Design Science Research
(DSR) [59] and provides artifacts to facilitate model-driven deployment of operational control DTs in the AECO domain. We
start with requirements elicitation (cf. Section III-A) and conclude with prototyping and experimentation (cf. Section V).



Our artifacts are developed in response to the following design
science problem [59]:


**Improve** _DT_ _deployment_ _in_ _BIM-based_ _infrastructure_
_projects_ (context)
**by designing** _a model-driven automation framework_ (artifact)
**that satisfies** _semantic integration, real-time connectivity, and_
_extensibility_ (requirement)
**in order to** _enable scalable DT engineering in the AECO_
_domain_ . (goal)


III. BIM- BASED D IGITAL T WINS

BIM is increasingly regarded as the principal modelling
paradigm in the AECO sector, providing a comprehensive
framework for generating, managing, and disseminating digital
building information throughout the entire building life cycle
with policy initiatives and regulatory mandates decisively
fostering and accelerating this development [60]. BIM enables
the creation of semantically rich, object-based 3D models that
incorporate geometric, spatial, and functional characteristics
of building components and their relationships. These models provide a collaborative platform where architects, engineers, and construction professionals can coordinate design
decisions, detect clashes, simulate performance, and generate
documentation [61].
While there are also DTs and industry-specific solutions
for the digital mapping of assets in other industries, such
as the manufacturing industry (e.g. capitalizing on Modelbased Systems Engineering; MBSE) [1], [62], BIM is becoming the standard solution in the construction industry [63],

[64]. Requirements regarding the complexity of construction
projects and availability over long building life cycles are
covered by BIM. It is therefore essential that concepts such as
DTs in the construction sector are also designed for longevity
and compatibility with existing, AECO-specific systems and
standards such as BIM.
Despite these strengths, BIM however exhibits significant
limitations when considering its application as a foundation
for DTs [7], [9], [14]. First, BIM models are predominantly
static representations focused on design and construction
phases, lacking mechanisms for real-time data integration from
operational buildings. Second, BIM tools typically provide
limited support for bidirectional communication with physical
systems, a core requirement for functional DTs. Third, while
BIM excels at representing physical and spatial aspects of
buildings, it lacks native capabilities for modeling runtime
aspects of Building Control Systems (BCS), essential for
operational monitoring and control [7], [11], [12], [21].
These limitations create a fundamental gap between BIM’s
design-time focus and the operational requirements of DTs.
DTs require continuous synchronization with physical assets
through sensor data, the ability to process and analyze this
data in real-time, and mechanisms to feed control signals
back to the BCS. In addition, DTs also require extensible
architectures that can accommodate evolving services and
analytics throughout the building life cycle [7].
Nevertheless, BIM provides an ideal semantic foundation
for DTs due to its comprehensive representation of building


geometry, topology, and component relationships. What is
needed is a systematic approach to transform these static
BIM models into dynamic, operational DTs by addressing the
identified limitations and extending BIM through a formal
methodology for modeling BCS runtime aspects and their
integration with a building’s digital representation.


_A. Uses Cases, Requirements, and Solution Proposal_


TABLE I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment
Framework


**Role** **Description**


Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates
DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

enabler for DTs, we have developed a comprehensive set of
requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS


Figure 2 (p. 7) outlines our solution proposal. Starting with a
BIM model which contains all relevant information pertaining
deployment, e.g., modeled devices and relevant middleware
metadata for Hono and Ditto (cf. Section IV-A for a detailed
discussion of the modeling and deployment methodology),
after validating the model [65], the model is fed into the
Hono/Ditto Configurator and scanned for ”twinnable”
devices to extract relevant metadata (e.g., tenant and device
ID or endpoint addresses) fo
